text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPLE-PHASE CONTRAST) AND PET-CT WHOLE BODY
Pre-Surgical Restaging Examination

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old female with biopsy-proven pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (moderately differentiated), initially staged as borderline resectable with superior mesenteric artery (SMA) abutment and superior mesenteric vein (SMV) involvement. Patient completed 8 cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy (modified FOLFIRINOX x 6 cycles followed by FOLFIRI x 2 cycles) with documented partial response on prior restaging CT. Subsequently completed long-course chemoradiation (IMRT, 54 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine as radiosensitizer), concluding approximately 5 weeks prior to this examination. Biliary stent in situ. Current CA 19-9: 52 U/mL. This study was ordered for pre-operative surgical planning and assessment of resectability.

--- CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL ---

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphase CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice MDCT scanner following administration of 120 mL of intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350). Imaging was acquired in the non-contrast, early arterial, late arterial (pancreatic parenchymal), and portal venous phases at 0.625 mm collimation with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations. Oral contrast was not administered. Pancreatic protocol technique was used with bolus tracking at the aortic level. Images were reviewed at standard soft tissue, liver, and lung windows.

FINDINGS:

Pancreas and Primary Tumor:
In the region of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, there is a persistent area of hypoenhancement relative to the adjacent pancreatic parenchyma, now measuring approximately 2.3 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 2.6 cm on the prior CT performed following completion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and 3.8 cm at baseline staging). The lesion demonstrates continued hypoenhancement across all phases with surrounding desmoplastic soft tissue reaction, which is expected following chemoradiation and is consistent with residual/treated primary tumor. The margins of the lesion are somewhat indistinct, which in the post-treatment setting reflects a combination of treatment effect, fibrosis, and possibly residual viable tumor — this is a characteristic appearance following neoadjuvant chemoradiation in pancreatic adenocarcinoma and does not necessarily indicate disease progression. No new areas of suspicious hypoenhancement within the gland are seen.

The upstream main pancreatic duct in the neck and body measures approximately 4.5 mm in caliber, representing improvement compared to the 7 mm dilation seen on the initial staging CT, consistent with partial decompression of the obstructing mass. A focal area of pancreatic parenchymal atrophy in the body and tail distal to the mass is noted, unchanged from prior and attributed to chronic ductal obstruction.

The peripancreatic soft tissue stranding in the region of the uncinate process is extensive and may represent a combination of desmoplastic reaction, post-radiation fibrosis, and residual disease; this finding is not meaningfully distinguishable from the prior post-chemotherapy restaging examination.

Vascular Relationships:
Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): The previously documented abutment of the primary mass against the SMA has continued to diminish with multimodality treatment. The soft tissue contact along the right lateral wall of the SMA is now estimated at less than 60 degrees of circumferential involvement (reduced from approximately 120 degrees following neoadjuvant chemotherapy, and approximately 180 degrees at baseline). There is no convincing evidence of arterial encasement, irregularity of the arterial wall, or loss of the fat plane along the majority of the SMA circumference. The SMA origin and proximal course are patent with no hemodynamically significant narrowing. This represents a meaningful improvement in the vascular interface and is consistent with an evolving response to multimodality therapy.

Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV) and Portal Vein: The SMV is now patent throughout its course, without evidence of focal narrowing or luminal compromise at the level of the pancreatic head mass. The previously described focal venous narrowing at the interface with the mass has resolved on the current examination. The portal confluence and main portal vein are widely patent. No venous thrombosis is identified.

Celiac Axis and Common Hepatic Artery: No abutment or involvement. The celiac axis and its major branches, including the proper hepatic artery, left gastric artery, and splenic artery, appear normal in course and caliber without peri-arterial soft tissue thickening.

Splenic Vein: Patent. No evidence of thrombosis.

Biliary System:
A fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS) is in place within the common bile duct, with the proximal end in the hepatic duct confluence and distal end extending into the duodenum. The stent is patent with appropriate luminal opacification. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is absent (post-ERCP / stent placement). There is no new biliary obstruction or evidence of cholangitis.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypodense or rim-enhancing lesions suspicious for metastatic disease. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously. There is no biliary dilation. No perihepatic fluid.

Lymph Nodes:
In the peripancreatic region, two small soft tissue density lymph nodes are again identified, the largest measuring approximately 0.7 cm in short axis (previously up to 0.9 cm at baseline). These nodes have decreased in size compared to initial staging and are not enlarged by size criteria on the current examination. No new pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the celiac, para-aortic, portacaval, or mesenteric distributions. No mediastinal or hilar adenopathy (assessed on lung windows).

Spleen:
Unremarkable. Normal size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilaterally unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or masses.

Kidneys:
Both kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric parenchymal enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. A 3 mm hypodense focus in the right renal cortex is too small to characterize but is statistically most likely a tiny cyst; unchanged from prior study.

Stomach and Bowel:
The stomach and proximal duodenum appear somewhat compressed by the mass effect from the region of the pancreatic head. The duodenum demonstrates mild wall thickening in the second portion adjacent to the mass, which may be secondary to post-radiation inflammatory change. The remainder of the small and large bowel is unremarkable without obstruction or perforation. No bowel wall thickening or pneumatosis.

Peritoneum and Retroperitoneum:
No ascites. No peritoneal soft tissue nodularity or implants. No omental thickening or cake formation. The retroperitoneum is unremarkable without bulky soft tissue masses.

Chest Bases (included on CT):
Small bilateral pleural effusions are not present. No lung nodules at the lung bases bilaterally. No consolidation.

Miscellaneous:
Surgical biliary stent in situ as described. No other surgical hardware. No pneumoperitoneum.

--- PET-CT (18F-FDG WHOLE BODY) ---

TECHNIQUE:
Whole body FDG-PET/CT was performed following intravenous administration of approximately 12.4 mCi of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG). The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to injection. Serum glucose at time of injection: 118 mg/dL (mildly elevated in the setting of known type 2 diabetes; acceptable for study interpretation). Imaging was performed from the skull base to mid-thighs at 90 minutes post-injection. Low-dose CT was used for attenuation correction and anatomical co-registration.

FINDINGS:

Primary Pancreatic Mass:
Moderately increased FDG avidity is identified in the region of the pancreatic head and uncinate process, co-localizing with the hypoenhancing mass on the concurrent CT. The maximum standardized uptake value (SUVmax) measures approximately 4.1. This is notably reduced compared to the SUVmax obtained on prior functional imaging at initial staging, in keeping with metabolic response to multimodality therapy. The area of residual FDG uptake is consistent with viable residual tumor in the context of ongoing treatment effect and fibrosis; however, treatment-related metabolic activity cannot be entirely excluded and is a recognized limitation of post-radiation FDG-PET interpretation. The overall pattern is most consistent with a partial metabolic response.

Liver:
No FDG-avid hepatic lesions. The liver background is physiologically homogeneous. No focal areas of abnormal tracer accumulation.

Lymph Nodes:
The peripancreatic lymph nodes, as described on CT, do not demonstrate FDG avidity above background. No pathologically FDG-avid lymph nodes in the mesenteric, celiac, retroperitoneal, or pelvic nodal stations. No mediastinal or hilar FDG-avid adenopathy.

Lung:
No FDG-avid pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural-based FDG avidity. The lung parenchyma appears clear without consolidation.

Bone:
No FDG-avid osseous lesions suspicious for skeletal metastatic disease. The axial and appendicular skeleton demonstrates normal background marrow activity. No focal lytic or sclerotic lesions on the concurrent CT to suggest bone involvement.

Pelvis:
No pelvic FDG-avid masses. The uterus and adnexa are not separately delineated; no definitive pelvic lesion.

Peritoneum:
No FDG-avid peritoneal implants. The peritoneal surfaces and omentum are metabolically quiescent.

Other:
Physiologic FDG activity in the brain, myocardium, liver, spleen, kidneys, ureters, and bladder. Biliary stent in situ without associated hypermetabolism. No unexpected sites of abnormal tracer accumulation.

IMPRESSION:

1. Primary pancreatic head / uncinate process mass: Further decrease in size since completion of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, now measuring approximately 2.3 cm (down from 2.6 cm post-chemotherapy and 3.8 cm at initial staging), representing a cumulative reduction of approximately 39% from baseline in response to multimodality neoadjuvant therapy. The degree of SMA contact continues to diminish and is now estimated at less than 60 degrees of circumferential abutment, without evidence of arterial encasement or wall irregularity. The SMV is patent throughout with resolution of the previously observed focal narrowing.

2. No hepatic metastases, peritoneal implants, or distant FDG-avid disease. No new suspicious lymphadenopathy.

3. Residual moderate FDG avidity in the primary mass (SUVmax 4.1) is decreased from prior staging, consistent with partial metabolic response; viable residual tumor and/or post-radiation inflammatory change are both contributory and cannot be reliably distinguished at this interval.

4. Patent biliary stent. No biliary obstruction.

5. Overall, the imaging findings are favorable for surgical planning. Vascular anatomy as described above should be reviewed carefully by the hepatobiliary surgical team in the context of operative planning, with specific attention to the SMA-tumor interface and the previously involved SMV segment.
